slides.title: Your First Collection of Things
slides.body: A Python **list** is an ordered collection of items you can change anytime. Think of it like a grocery list — you can add, remove, and reorder items whenever you like.
slides.tip: Lists are one of the most useful tools in all of Python — you'll use them constantly.

slides.title: Building a List
slides.body: Wrap items in **square brackets** `[]` and separate them with commas. Lists can hold any type — strings, numbers, booleans — even mixed together.

slides.title: Indexing Starts at Zero
slides.body: Every item has a **position number** called an index. Python counts from `0`, so the first item is `list[0]`. Use **negative indexes** like `-1` to count from the end.
slides.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print(fruits[0])   # first item
print(fruits[-1])  # last item
slides.tip: Reaching for index 1 when you want the first item is the #1 beginner mistake.

slides.title: Lists are Mutable — Change Them!
slides.body: Lists are **mutable**, meaning you can change them after creation. Use `.append()` to add to the end, `.remove()` to delete a value, and assign to an index to replace an item.
slides.code:
tasks = ["wash dishes", "buy milk"]
tasks.append("call mom")
print(tasks)
print(len(tasks))
slides.tip: Use `len(my_list)` to count items, and `item in my_list` to check if something exists.

slides.title: Watch Out: Copying a List
slides.body: Writing `b = a` does **not** copy the list — both variables point to the **same** list. To get an independent copy, use `a.copy()` or `a[:]`.
slides.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a.copy()  # real copy
b.append(99)
print(a)  # a is unchanged
slides.tip: Forgetting .copy() is a classic gotcha that surprises almost every beginner.

slides.title: You've Got Lists!
slides.body: You can now create lists, access items by index, modify them with methods like `.append()` and `.sort()`, loop through them with `for`, and safely copy them. Lists unlock a huge range of real programs.
slides.tip: Next up: put lists to work in loops and build your first real data-driven programs.
